summary: This notebook demonstrates a baseline submission for the FIDE & Google Efficient Chess AI Challenge by integrating a lightweight C++ chess engine (Micromax/MCU-Max) into the Kaggle chess environment. The author compiles the engine from source, wraps it in a Python class using the UCI protocol for move generation, and handles a known pawn promotion edge case. It serves as a practical guide for leveraging external compiled binaries in Kaggle game environments without relying on heavy ML frameworks. Readers learn how to bridge C++ game logic with Python submission agents and optimize for strict time/memory constraints.
competition_flow: Clones and compiles a C++ chess engine, wraps it in a Python UCI interface to process FEN board states, and packages the agent for Kaggle game submission.
data_reading: Parses board state from FEN strings provided in the `obs['board']` field by the Kaggle chess environment.
data_processing: None; relies on the engine's native FEN parsing and move generation.
insights: Wrapping a compiled C/C++ binary via the UCI protocol is a viable and efficient strategy for game AI submissions in Kaggle.
Explicitly handling pawn promotion moves (e.g., appending 'q') is necessary when the underlying engine or FEN parser does not auto-complete them.
Lightweight, pre-compiled engines can outperform heavy ML models in time-constrained game environments.
